news_summary: New scientific insights into ocean currents, innovative use of electric vehicles to strengthen the power grid, official naming and protection efforts for 24 species, and nature-inspired engineering to prevent flooding all highlight progress in environmental understanding and conservation.
news_summary_fr: Les nouvelles découvertes scientifiques sur les courants océaniques, l'utilisation innovante des véhicules électriques pour renforcer le réseau électrique, les efforts officiels visant à nommer et à protéger 24 espèces, ainsi que l'ingénierie inspirée de la nature pour prévenir les inondations, sont autant d'exemples qui illustrent les progrès réalisés en matière de compréhension et de conservation de l'environnement.
news_summary_es: Los nuevos avances científicos sobre las corrientes oceánicas, el uso innovador de los vehículos eléctricos para reforzar la red eléctrica, las iniciativas oficiales de denominación y protección de 24 especies, y la ingeniería inspirada en la naturaleza para prevenir las inundaciones ponen de relieve los avances en la comprensión y la conservación del medio ambiente.

articles.title: Earth’s most powerful ocean current didn’t form the way we thought
articles.summary: A colossal ocean current encircling Antarctica—stronger than all the world’s rivers combined—played a far more complex role in shaping Earth’s climate than scientists once thought. New research shows it didn’t form just because ocean gateways opened, but required shifting continents and powerful winds to align. This shift helped pull carbon dioxide out of the atmosphere, contributing to a major cooling event that transformed Earth into the ice-covered world we know today.
articles.content:
The Antarctic Circumpolar Current carries more than 100 times the total flow of all the world's rivers combined. It circles Antarctica without being blocked by land, making it one of the most important drivers of the global climate system. New research published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences explores how and when this immense current first developed. The findings reveal that simply opening ocean gateways between Antarctica, South America, and Australia was not enough to create it.

Around 34 million years ago, Earth experienced a dramatic transformation during the transition into the Oligocene -- shifting from a warm greenhouse world with little ice to a cooler icehouse climate marked by expanding polar ice sheets. During this period, ocean passages between Antarctica, Australia, and South America widened and deepened. At the same time, the Antarctic Circumpolar Current (ACC) began to take shape, and the Antarctic Ice Sheet started forming.

Atmospheric CO 2 levels were about 600 ppm back then. This level has not been reached again since, although some future climate scenarios suggest it could be surpassed by the end of this century. "In order to predict the possible future climate, it is necessary to look into the past with simulations and data to understand our Earth in warmer and more CO 2 -rich climate states than today," says Hanna Knahl, climate modeller at the Alfred Wegener Institute, Helmholtz Centre for Polar and Marine Research (AWI) and lead author of the study, which now appears in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS). "But careful, the climate of the past can of course not be projected 1:1 onto the future. Our study shows that the circumpolar current in its 'infancy' influenced the climate very differently than today's fully developed ACC does."

Reconstructing the Birth of the Antarctic Circumpolar Current

To understand how the ACC formed, Knahl and her team ran detailed climate simulations based on Earth's geography about 33.5 million years ago, when Australia and South America were positioned much closer to Antarctica. The researchers combined these simulations with an Antarctic Ice Sheet model from a 2024 Science study, linking it with ocean, atmosphere, and land systems to track how currents evolved.

The modeled results were then compared with geological reconstructions from the same time period, allowing the team to test how well their simulations matched real-world evidence.

The Key Role of Winds and Shifting Continents

The results highlight the importance of the Tasman Gateway, a seaway between Antarctica and Australia. "There were already indications that the wind in the Tasman Gateway played an important role in the formation of the ACC. Our simulations can clearly confirm this: Only when Australia had moved further away from Antarctica and the strong westerly winds blew directly through the Tasman Gateway, the current could fully develop," Knahl explains.

The study also suggests that the Southern Ocean looked very different during this early stage. Even though ocean passages were already open, the current did not yet form a continuous loop. Instead, strong flow developed in the Atlantic and Indian regions, while the Pacific sector remained relatively calm.

Advanced Simulations Reveal New Insights

Coupling climate and ice sheet models is still a relatively new and complex approach, but it allows scientists to capture interactions between different parts of the Earth system more realistically. For this work, researchers from AWI's Palaeoclimate Dynamics and Marine Geology divisions collaborated with international partners, including the Australian Centre of Excellence in Antarctic Science and the Antarctic Research Centre Wellington.

"With this PNAS study, we are showing -- for the first time -- how helpful and important it is to carry out these coupled and relatively high-resolution model simulations for the climate of the deep past. Even though they are very demanding, they provide novel insights into the interaction of ice, atmosphere, land surface, and ocean," explains AWI palaeoclimate modeller Prof Dr Gerrit Lohmann, co-author of the study.

Why the Antarctic Current Matters for Today's Climate

By reconstructing the formation of the ACC, the researchers were able to show how global ocean circulation was reorganized in Earth's past. This shift had major consequences for the planet's climate system. According to AWI geoscientist Dr Johann Klages, "This understanding is crucial, as the formation of the ACC has strongly driven carbon uptake by the ocean. This reduction in the concentration of greenhouse gases in Earth's atmosphere thus had the potential to initiate the cooler climate of the so-called Cenozoic Ice Age, which continues to this day with permanently ice-covered polar ice caps, in which warm and cold periods alternate. This new knowledge will therefore help us to more reliably interpret recent changes in Southern Ocean circulation more reliably."

These findings provide a clearer picture of how ocean currents, atmospheric conditions, and shifting continents worked together to reshape Earth's climate, offering valuable context for understanding future changes.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding the formation of the Antarctic Circumpolar Current, a major driver of Earth's climate system. This research has broad implications for climate science and helps improve predictions of future climate change, offering meaningful benefits to society by enhancing our understanding of carbon cycling and global cooling mechanisms. The article provides detailed context on the research methods, findings, and their importance, satisfying the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: New research reveals how Antarctica’s powerful ocean current shaped Earth’s climate
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Antarctic Circumpolar Current (ACC) is the world’s strongest ocean current, flowing around Antarctica and moving more water than all the world’s rivers combined. Scientists have long known it plays a big role in Earth’s climate but were unsure exactly how it first formed.

**What happened** – A team of researchers used detailed computer models and geological data to study the ACC’s beginnings about 34 million years ago. They found that the current didn’t form just because ocean passages opened between Antarctica, South America, and Australia. Instead, it required the continents to shift farther apart and strong winds to blow through these new ocean gateways, especially the Tasman Gateway between Antarctica and Australia.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how scientists understand the ACC’s role in pulling carbon dioxide out of the atmosphere. That process helped cool the planet and led to the growth of Antarctica’s ice sheets, which shaped the cooler climate we live in today. It also shows how complex Earth’s climate system is and how land, ocean, and air work together.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to use these advanced models to better understand how ocean currents might change in the future as the planet warms. This knowledge could improve climate predictions and help us understand how carbon moves between the ocean and atmosphere.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have uncovered that Antarctica’s giant ocean current formed through a unique mix of shifting continents and winds, which helped cool Earth and create today’s icy poles.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle recherche révèle comment le puissant courant océanique de l’Antarctique a façonné le climat de la Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le courant circumpolaire antarctique (CCA) est le courant océanique le plus puissant au monde, circulant autour de l’Antarctique et transportant plus d’eau que toutes les rivières du monde réunies. Les scientifiques savaient qu’il jouait un grand rôle dans le climat de la Terre, mais ignoraient comment il s’était formé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs a utilisé des modèles informatiques détaillés et des données géologiques pour étudier les débuts du CCA il y a environ 34 millions d’années. Ils ont découvert que le courant ne s’est pas formé simplement parce que des passages océaniques se sont ouverts entre l’Antarctique, l’Amérique du Sud et l’Australie. Il a fallu que les continents s’éloignent et que de forts vents soufflent à travers ces nouvelles voies marines, surtout le détroit de Tasman entre l’Antarctique et l’Australie.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la compréhension scientifique du rôle du CCA dans l’absorption du dioxyde de carbone de l’atmosphère. Ce processus a aidé à refroidir la planète et à faire grandir les calottes glaciaires de l’Antarctique, façonnant ainsi le climat plus froid que nous connaissons aujourd’hui. Elle montre aussi la complexité du système climatique terrestre et comment la terre, l’océan et l’air interagissent.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’utiliser ces modèles avancés pour mieux comprendre comment les courants océaniques pourraient évoluer avec le réchauffement climatique. Ces connaissances pourraient améliorer les prévisions climatiques et expliquer comment le carbone circule entre l’océan et l’atmosphère.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que le gigantesque courant océanique de l’Antarctique s’est formé grâce à une combinaison unique de mouvements continentaux et de vents, ce qui a aidé à refroidir la Terre et à créer les pôles glacés d’aujourd’hui.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva investigación revela cómo la poderosa corriente oceánica de la Antártida moldeó el clima de la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Corriente Circumpolar Antártica (CCA) es la corriente oceánica más fuerte del mundo, que fluye alrededor de la Antártida y mueve más agua que todos los ríos del mundo juntos. Los científicos sabían que tenía un gran papel en el clima de la Tierra, pero no cómo se formó.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores usó modelos por computadora y datos geológicos para estudiar el inicio de la CCA hace unos 34 millones de años. Descubrieron que la corriente no se formó solo porque se abrieron pasajes oceánicos entre la Antártida, Sudamérica y Australia. Fue necesario que los continentes se separaran más y que fuertes vientos soplaran por estas nuevas rutas, especialmente por el Estrecho de Tasmania entre la Antártida y Australia.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo cambia cómo los científicos entienden el papel de la CCA en sacar dióxido de carbono de la atmósfera. Ese proceso ayudó a enfriar el planeta y a que crecieran las capas de hielo de la Antártida, formando el clima frío que conocemos hoy. También muestra lo complejo que es el sistema climático de la Tierra y cómo la tierra, el océano y el aire trabajan juntos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar estos modelos avanzados para entender mejor cómo podrían cambiar las corrientes oceánicas con el calentamiento global. Esto puede ayudar a predecir el clima y entender mejor cómo se mueve el carbono entre el océano y la atmósfera.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos descubrieron que la gran corriente oceánica de la Antártida se formó gracias a una combinación única de movimientos continentales y vientos, lo que ayudó a enfriar la Tierra y crear los polos helados actuales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast, swirling ocean current encircling a stylized, icy Antarctic landmass, with gently shifting continental silhouettes of South America and Australia nearby; strong, flowing wind currents are symbolized by graceful, sweeping lines passing through a narrow seaway between Antarctica and Australia, while the ocean shows contrasting dynamic waves in the Atlantic and Indian sectors and calmer waters in the Pacific, all rendered in natural, soft blues, whites, and earth tones.

articles.title: How EVs could solve a problem with America’s rickety grid
articles.content:
There’s a technology sitting idle in garages and driveways across America that provides a solution to its own potential problem. As more and more electric vehicles tap the grid, their giant batteries add to the system’s load. Timing is also a challenge: When people get home from work and plug their cars in, so too is everyone elsewhere switching on their own appliances, like washing machines and ovens and such.

But instead of being burdens to the electrical system, a clever trick is putting EVs on a trajectory to help save it. More models feature the ability to send their energy back to the grid in times of high demand — a trick known as vehicle-to-grid, or V2G — forming a vast network of backup power across a city. As demand wanes through the night, they charge up, ensuring an EV owner has enough juice to get to work in the morning.

However, a new study warns that for V2G to fully compensate for all those batteries plugging in, the technology needs an assist, in the form of infrastructural improvements like new transformers and transmission lines. That will create a more resilient system and encourage the growth of renewables. “You have to upgrade your power system as soon as possible,” said Ziyou Song, an energy systems engineer at the University of Michigan and co-author of a new paper describing the findings. “V2G is really helpful, for sure — 100 percent. But just to some extent, V2G itself cannot resolve the charging demand of so many electric vehicles in the future.”

For this study, the researchers modeled scenarios for the San Francisco Bay Area, projecting how quickly EVs and solar power will be adopted — that is, how much demand will be put on the grid as renewable energy increases. Drilling deeper, they also projected where and when EVs might charge. (As with any modeling, there are some uncertainties here: EV adoption might happen slower or faster than expected, for example. The loss of federal tax credits for buying the vehicles might be reducing demand, but on the other hand, the gasoline price shock from the Iran war might drive more folks to go electric.) They also considered what it would cost to upgrade the grid over the same period.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

All told, the modeling found that the cheapest option is to proactively upgrade the grid in anticipation of these changes, instead of doing so in phases over time in reaction to them. Then, as more EVs plug in, the vehicles will be able to draw enough power without the system straining. And with V2G, they’ll form a fleet of batteries that grid operators can tap to meet demand. In other words: EVs can help stabilize the grid, so long as they’re equipped with the technology to provide power in addition to taking it. “V2G plus the proactive power system upgrade will address the entire issue,” Song said.

This, in turn, can help smooth the “intermittency” challenge of renewables. Any grid must constantly balance the amount of electricity it’s generating with what its customers need at any given moment. With fossil fuels, utilities can just burn more gas or coal as demand rises. But renewable energy works differently, because the sun isn’t always shining and the wind isn’t always blowing. That’s why utilities are investing in batteries that store that power for later use: At one point late last month, they met 43 percent of demand in California, or six times the output of Hoover Dam.

The promise of V2G isn’t that it will replace battery farms, but instead essentially break them up into smaller ones spread across town. If the sun goes down at 5 p.m. when everyone is getting home and demand is rising, a utility can call on its battery facilities, but also on EVs, to send electricity into the system. (Anyone participating in the program would be paid for that juice.) Alternatively, those vehicles can electrify individual homes, divorcing those abodes from the grid, further reducing overall demand. All of this is good for EV owners, too, as they’re not drawing electricity when it’s most expensive. It wouldn’t just be passenger vehicles, either: Pilot projects are turning electric school buses — and their jumbo batteries — into reliable assets for the grid.

In these early days of V2G, utilities are still working out how to incentivize EV owners to participate, and how much to compensate them for sending power to the grid. The idea is to reach a sort of critical mass, where there’s enough people involved that it won’t matter if some folks choose to opt out. “When you’re operating 3,000, 30,000, 300,000, then any individual customers having different behavior won’t matter,” said Chris Rauscher, vice president and head of grid services at the battery storage and solar company Sunrun, which has been running V2G pilot projects.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The idea is to turn a vehicle from a depreciating asset into a source of income for the owner. One wrinkle, though, is that V2G could reduce the lifetime of a battery, due to the extra cycles of charging and discharging. Still, utilities are already repurposing old EV batteries — which need to be replaced when they drop to 70 to 80 percent of their original capacity — as stationary assets on the grid. “That’s a good way to keep getting value out of them,” said Patricia Hidalgo-Gonzalez, director of the Renewable Energy and Advanced Mathematics Laboratory at the University of California San Diego, who studies the grid but wasn’t involved in the new paper. “The program could even swap the battery for the EV owner. So, say, if you sign up for this pilot where you provide V2G services, after three years, we replace your battery with a new one.”

This tech can be paired with another powerful technique for supporting the grid, known as active managed charging. This opt-in program uses algorithms to stagger when EVs charge at night, instead of them all drawing power at 5 pm. When participants get home, they plug in, but the electrons might not flow until midnight, when most folks are asleep and not using much energy. The system also recognizes when an EV owner leaves for work in the morning, and how much battery they need, so charging switches on with enough time to spare.

Still, even combined, active managed charging and V2G alone can’t fix the grid of tomorrow. “We have to upgrade our power system as soon as possible,” Song said, “because V2G is not a silver bullet.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes how electric vehicles (EVs) with vehicle-to-grid (V2G) technology can help stabilize and support the electrical grid, enabling better integration of renewable energy and reducing strain during peak demand. It highlights a proactive approach to upgrading grid infrastructure, which has broad positive implications for energy resilience, sustainability, and climate action. The story is focused, detailed, and addresses a significant environmental and societal challenge with a promising technological and policy solution.
articles.personality_title: Electric vehicles could help strengthen America’s power grid
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As more people in the United States start using electric vehicles (EVs), these cars need to charge from the electrical grid. This adds extra demand to a power system that is already old and sometimes unreliable, especially during busy times when many people use electricity at once.

**What happened** – Researchers studied how EVs with a technology called vehicle-to-grid (V2G) can send electricity back to the grid when demand is high. This means EVs don’t just take power; they can also supply it. The study focused on the San Francisco Bay Area and found that upgrading the grid’s infrastructure—like transformers and power lines—alongside using V2G, is the best way to keep the system stable as more EVs and solar energy are used.

**Impact** – This is important because it turns EVs into a network of mobile batteries that help balance electricity needs. When the sun goes down and people come home, EVs can give power back to the grid, reducing blackouts and making it easier to use renewable energy like solar and wind. It also means EV owners can save money by charging when electricity is cheaper and even earn money by sharing power.

**What's next step** – Utilities and companies are working on ways to encourage more EV owners to join V2G programs by offering payments and replacing batteries when needed. They are also improving how EVs charge overnight to avoid too many cars charging at once. However, experts say that updating the power grid itself is still necessary to handle future demand.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Electric vehicles with the right technology can help support and strengthen the power grid, but upgrading the grid’s infrastructure is essential for the future of clean energy.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les véhicules électriques pourraient renforcer le réseau électrique américain
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De plus en plus d'Américains utilisent des véhicules électriques (VE), qui doivent se recharger sur le réseau électrique. Cela ajoute une demande supplémentaire à un système souvent ancien et parfois fragile, surtout aux heures de pointe.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié comment les VE équipés de la technologie véhicule-vers-réseau (V2G) peuvent renvoyer de l'électricité au réseau quand la demande est forte. Cette étude, réalisée dans la région de la baie de San Francisco, montre que moderniser le réseau (transformateurs, lignes électriques) en même temps que l'utilisation du V2G est la meilleure solution pour garder le système stable avec plus de VE et d’énergie solaire.

**Impact** – Cela permet de transformer les VE en batteries mobiles qui aident à équilibrer la demande d’électricité. Quand le soleil se couche et que tout le monde rentre chez soi, les VE peuvent fournir de l’électricité au réseau, réduisant les risques de coupures et facilitant l’usage des énergies renouvelables. Les propriétaires peuvent aussi économiser de l’argent en rechargeant quand l’électricité est moins chère, voire en gagnant de l’argent en partageant leur énergie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les compagnies électriques cherchent à encourager les propriétaires de VE à rejoindre les programmes V2G en proposant des paiements et en remplaçant les batteries si besoin. Elles améliorent aussi la gestion des recharges nocturnes pour éviter que trop de voitures se branchent en même temps. Mais les experts rappellent que moderniser le réseau reste indispensable.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les véhicules électriques équipés de la technologie V2G peuvent soutenir et renforcer le réseau électrique, mais une mise à jour des infrastructures est essentielle pour l’avenir de l’énergie propre.
articles.personality_title_es: Los vehículos eléctricos podrían fortalecer la red eléctrica de Estados Unidos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada vez más personas en Estados Unidos usan vehículos eléctricos (VE), que necesitan cargarse desde la red eléctrica. Esto añade más demanda a un sistema que muchas veces es viejo y puede fallar, especialmente en horas de mucha actividad.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron cómo los VE con tecnología vehículo-a-red (V2G) pueden enviar electricidad de vuelta a la red cuando la demanda es alta. El estudio, centrado en el área de la Bahía de San Francisco, encontró que actualizar la infraestructura del sistema eléctrico—como transformadores y líneas—junto con usar V2G, es la mejor forma de mantener la red estable con más VE y energía solar.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque convierte a los VE en una red de baterías móviles que ayudan a equilibrar la electricidad que se necesita. Cuando se pone el sol y todos llegan a casa, los VE pueden devolver energía a la red, reduciendo apagones y facilitando el uso de energías renovables. Además, los dueños de VE pueden ahorrar dinero cargando cuando la electricidad es más barata e incluso ganar dinero compartiendo energía.

**Próximo paso** – Las compañías eléctricas trabajan para motivar a más dueños de VE a unirse a programas V2G ofreciendo pagos y reemplazando baterías cuando sea necesario. También mejoran cómo se cargan los VE durante la noche para evitar que muchos se conecten a la vez. Sin embargo, los expertos dicen que es necesario actualizar el sistema eléctrico para manejar la demanda futura.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los vehículos eléctricos con la tecnología adecuada pueden ayudar a sostener y fortalecer la red eléctrica, pero actualizar la infraestructura es clave para el futuro de la energía limpia.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a peaceful neighborhood at dusk where stylized electric vehicles with glowing, softly pulsing battery icons are connected by gentle, flowing energy lines to a sturdy, glowing power grid transformer, symbolizing a harmonious exchange of energy; the scene includes subtle silhouettes of homes with softly lit windows and rooftop solar panels, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues to evoke calm and resilience.

articles.title: 24 creatures get their first names and a shot at being protected
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM In February 2024, sixteen scientists gathered at the University of Lodz in Poland, surrounded by snow, to spend a week examining creatures from the floor of the Pacific Ocean. The animals they were studying lived at depths of around 13,000 feet (roughly 4,000 meters), in near-total darkness, in a […]
The post 24 creatures get their first names and a shot at being protected first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

In February 2024, sixteen scientists gathered at the University of Lodz in Poland, surrounded by snow, to spend a week examining creatures from the floor of the Pacific Ocean.

The animals they were studying lived at depths of around 13,000 feet (roughly 4,000 meters), in near-total darkness, in a stretch of ocean between Hawaii and Mexico called the Clarion-Clipperton Zone. They were amphipods, shrimp-like organisms about one centimeter long, and none of them had ever been formally named. That was the whole point of the workshop. To name them.

Why naming matters

Taxonomy, the scientific process of identifying, describing, and classifying species, exists as its own discipline for a reason. A species that has not been formally named cannot be cited in research, cannot be listed as threatened or endangered, and cannot be included in conservation or policy frameworks.

“Until they are properly named for science in this official way, they are not communicable about,” says Tammy Horton of the National Oceanography Centre, one of the lead researchers. “It absolutely gives them a passport to be discussed, to be talked about, to be conserved.”

Dr. Anna Żażdżewska of the University of Lodz, who co-led the project, described naming a species as offering it “a passport for living.”

The team published 24 new species in the journal ZooKeys on March 24, along with two new genera and, perhaps most dramatically, an entirely new family and superfamily of crustaceans. A new superfamily is a new branch on the evolutionary tree.

“To find a new superfamily is incredibly exciting, and very rarely happens,” Horton said. She compared the discovery to finding dogs in a world where science had only catalogued bears and cats. She called it the most exciting find of her career.

The names they chose

The naming choices say something about the people behind them. Mirabestia maisie, the type species for the new superfamily, was named after Horton’s daughter. Byblis hortonae and Byblisoides jazdzewskae honored the lead researchers. Thrombasia ania carried a form of Żażdżewska’s first name. Eperopeus vermiculatus was named for the World Register of Marine Species. Lepidepecreum myla was named after a video game character.

And then there is Pseudolepechinella apricity. The word “apricity” refers to the warmth of winter sun on a cold day. Horton chose it for the atmosphere of the workshop itself: sixteen scientists from eight institutions, working together in a Polish February. “It was very apt,” she said, “as we discussed our findings in the warmth of the February sun amid the snow of the Polish winter in Lodz.”

The timing

Before this study, only 13 amphipod species had been formally described from the entire Clarion-Clipperton Zone. Scientists estimate more than 5,500 species exist in the region, with roughly 90 percent still unnamed.

The zone also sits atop large deposits of manganese nodules, formations rich in nickel, cobalt, and copper used in battery technology and renewable energy infrastructure. In January 2026, U.S. regulators changed rules to accelerate deep-sea mining permits. In March 2026, an application was accepted to begin mining more than 25,000 square miles of the same region.

Following machinery tests in the Clarion-Clipperton Zone in 2022, species abundance dropped 37 percent and biodiversity fell by nearly a third within two months, according to sediment analysis by the UK Natural History Museum.

“We’ve just done 24,” Horton noted, “and that is a drop in the ocean, literally, of how many more we have to describe.”

Source study: ZooKey’s — New deep-sea Amphipoda from the Clarion-Clipperton Zone: 24 new species described under the Sustainable Seabed Knowledge Initiative: One Thousand Reasons campaign

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific achievement of formally naming 24 new deep-sea species, including a new superfamily, which enables their recognition in conservation and policy frameworks. This has broad environmental significance given the threats from deep-sea mining in the Clarion-Clipperton Zone, a critical area for biodiversity and renewable energy resources. The detailed context about the species, their discovery, and the urgent need for protection provides substantial substance and real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists name 24 new deep-sea creatures to help protect them
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In early 2024, scientists studied tiny animals living about 13,000 feet deep in the Pacific Ocean, in an area called the Clarion-Clipperton Zone. This zone is important because it holds many unknown species and valuable minerals used in batteries and clean energy.

**What happened** – Sixteen scientists met in Poland for a week to officially name 24 new species of small shrimp-like creatures called amphipods. They also discovered a completely new family and superfamily of these animals, which is very rare. Naming these species means they can now be included in research and conservation efforts.

**Impact** – Naming species is important because unnamed animals cannot be protected or studied properly. This discovery adds to the small number of known species in a region where many remain unknown. It also comes at a time when deep-sea mining is increasing, which threatens these creatures’ habitats. By giving them names, scientists give these animals a chance to be recognized and saved.

**What’s next step** – Researchers hope to continue exploring and naming more species in the area, since only a tiny fraction of the estimated 5,500 species have been described. Their work supports efforts to protect the ocean floor from damage caused by mining and helps guide future policies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Giving names to 24 newly discovered deep-sea creatures helps scientists protect them in a region at risk from mining activities.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques nomment 24 nouvelles créatures des profondeurs pour mieux les protéger
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Début 2024, des scientifiques ont étudié de petits animaux vivant à environ 4 000 mètres de profondeur dans l’océan Pacifique, dans une zone appelée Clarion-Clipperton. Cette zone est importante car elle abrite de nombreuses espèces inconnues et des minéraux précieux utilisés dans les batteries et les énergies propres.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Seize scientifiques se sont réunis en Pologne pendant une semaine pour nommer officiellement 24 nouvelles espèces de petites créatures ressemblant à des crevettes, appelées amphipodes. Ils ont aussi découvert une nouvelle famille et superfamille, ce qui est très rare. Nommer ces espèces permet maintenant de les inclure dans la recherche et la protection.

**Impact** – Nommer une espèce est essentiel car sans nom, un animal ne peut pas être protégé ni étudié correctement. Cette découverte ajoute au petit nombre d’espèces connues dans une région où beaucoup restent inconnues. Elle intervient aussi alors que l’exploitation minière en haute mer augmente, menaçant ces habitats. Donner un nom, c’est offrir une chance d’être reconnu et sauvé.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent continuer à explorer et nommer d’autres espèces, car seulement une petite partie des 5 500 espèces estimées a été décrite. Leur travail soutient la protection du fond océanique face aux risques miniers et aide à orienter les futures politiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Nommer 24 nouvelles créatures des profondeurs aide à les protéger dans une région menacée par l’exploitation minière.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos nombran 24 nuevas criaturas del fondo marino para protegerlas mejor
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A principios de 2024, científicos estudiaron pequeños animales que viven a unos 4,000 metros de profundidad en el Océano Pacífico, en una zona llamada Clarion-Clipperton. Esta zona es importante porque alberga muchas especies desconocidas y minerales valiosos usados en baterías y energía limpia.

**Qué pasó** – Dieciséis científicos se reunieron en Polonia durante una semana para nombrar oficialmente 24 nuevas especies de pequeñas criaturas parecidas a camarones, llamadas anfípodos. También descubrieron una nueva familia y superfamilia, algo muy raro. Nombrar estas especies permite incluirlas en investigaciones y esfuerzos de conservación.

**Impacto** – Nombrar una especie es importante porque sin nombre, un animal no puede ser protegido ni estudiado correctamente. Este hallazgo suma a las pocas especies conocidas en una región donde muchas siguen sin describirse. Además, ocurre justo cuando la minería en aguas profundas aumenta, poniendo en riesgo estos hábitats. Darles nombre es darles oportunidad de ser reconocidas y protegidas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan seguir explorando y nombrando más especies, ya que solo una pequeña parte de las aproximadamente 5,500 especies estimadas ha sido descrita. Su trabajo apoya la protección del fondo marino frente a los daños de la minería y ayuda a guiar futuras políticas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Nombrar 24 nuevas criaturas del fondo marino ayuda a protegerlas en una región amenazada por la minería.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a deep ocean floor scene showing small, shrimp-like amphipods clustered around glowing manganese nodules, with a symbolic open book resting on the seabed emitting soft light that illuminates the creatures, while gentle snowflakes fall above a distant, stylized outline of a snowy Polish university building under a pale winter sun.

articles.title: Engineers are imitating beavers to prevent flooding
articles.summary: What do beavers have to do with preventing flooding? The aquatic rodents have become a source of inspiration for engineers looking to flood–proof rural areas. Beaver dams are one of the nature-based solutions being considered by the European Union in a €15 million project. The Lèze valley in the south of France is putting them to the test.
articles.content:
Engineers are imitating beavers to prevent flooding

Environment

DOWN TO EARTH © FRANCE 24

Share

From the show

Reading time 1 min

What do beavers have to do with preventing flooding? The aquatic rodents have become a source of inspiration for engineers looking to flood–proof rural areas. Beaver dams are one of the nature-based solutions being considered by the European Union in a €15 million project. The Lèze valley in the south of France is putting them to the test.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a nature-based engineering solution inspired by beaver dams to prevent flooding, which has broad environmental and societal benefits. It involves a significant EU-funded project with real-world application in rural areas, demonstrating meaningful progress in climate adaptation and flood management.
articles.personality_title: Engineers use beaver dam ideas to help stop floods
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Flooding can cause serious problems in rural areas, damaging homes, farms, and nature. Engineers are looking for new ways to protect these places from floods.

**What happened** – In the south of France, engineers are copying how beavers build dams to control water. This idea is part of a big project funded by the European Union with €15 million. The project is testing beaver-inspired dams in the Lèze valley to see if they can reduce flooding.

**Impact** – Beaver dams naturally slow down water flow and hold back water during heavy rains. Using these natural designs can help protect land without harmful chemicals or big machines. This approach shows how working with nature can solve problems and help communities stay safe.

**What's next step** – If the tests in the Lèze valley work well, this method could be used in other flood-prone rural areas across Europe. The project might also study how to build these dams effectively and how they affect local wildlife.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Inspired by beavers, engineers are testing natural dams to better protect rural areas from flooding in a major European project.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des ingénieurs s’inspirent des barrages de castors pour prévenir les inondations
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les inondations peuvent causer de gros dégâts dans les zones rurales, touchant les maisons, les fermes et la nature. Les ingénieurs cherchent de nouvelles solutions pour protéger ces zones.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Dans le sud de la France, des ingénieurs imitent la façon dont les castors construisent leurs barrages pour contrôler l’eau. Cette idée fait partie d’un grand projet financé par l’Union européenne avec 15 millions d’euros. Le projet teste ces barrages inspirés des castors dans la vallée de la Lèze pour voir s’ils réduisent les inondations.

**Impact** – Les barrages de castors ralentissent naturellement le débit de l’eau et retiennent l’eau lors des fortes pluies. Utiliser ces solutions naturelles peut protéger les terres sans produits chimiques ni gros engins. Cette méthode montre comment la nature peut aider à résoudre des problèmes et protéger les communautés.

**Prochaines étapes** – Si les tests dans la vallée de la Lèze réussissent, cette méthode pourrait être utilisée dans d’autres zones rurales inondables en Europe. Le projet pourrait aussi étudier comment construire ces barrages efficacement et leur impact sur la faune locale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Inspirés par les castors, des ingénieurs testent des barrages naturels pour mieux protéger les zones rurales des inondations dans un grand projet européen.
articles.personality_title_es: Ingenieros usan ideas de presas de castores para prevenir inundaciones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las inundaciones pueden causar problemas graves en áreas rurales, dañando casas, granjas y la naturaleza. Los ingenieros buscan nuevas formas de proteger estas zonas.

**Qué pasó** – En el sur de Francia, ingenieros están copiando cómo los castores construyen presas para controlar el agua. Esta idea forma parte de un gran proyecto financiado por la Unión Europea con 15 millones de euros. El proyecto está probando estas presas inspiradas en castores en el valle de Lèze para ver si pueden reducir las inundaciones.

**Impacto** – Las presas de castores ralentizan naturalmente el flujo del agua y retienen agua durante lluvias fuertes. Usar estos diseños naturales puede ayudar a proteger la tierra sin químicos ni maquinaria pesada. Este enfoque muestra cómo trabajar con la naturaleza puede resolver problemas y mantener seguras a las comunidades.

**Próximos pasos** – Si las pruebas en el valle de Lèze funcionan bien, este método podría usarse en otras áreas rurales con riesgo de inundaciones en Europa. El proyecto también podría estudiar cómo construir estas presas de forma efectiva y cómo afectan a la vida silvestre local.

**Resumen en una frase** – Inspirados por los castores, ingenieros prueban presas naturales para proteger mejor las áreas rurales de las inundaciones en un gran proyecto europeo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a serene river valley in southern France featuring a stylized beaver dam gently holding back water, with flowing streams weaving through lush greenery and soft hills under a calm sky, symbolizing nature-inspired engineering to prevent flooding.
